scenarios.name: Specific Performance of Sale Agreement

checks.detail: Specific Relief Act / specific performance cited
At least one statutory section cited
Prayer for execution of sale deed
No invented amounts (10L/50L never in facts)

scenarios.draft_text:
IN THE CITY CIVIL COURT AT BENGALURU (KARNATAKA)

SUIT NO. ___ OF 2024

BETWEEN:

PLAINTIFF:
SMT. ANITA SHARMA,
Aged 52 years,
Occupation: {{PLACEHOLDER_OCCUPATION}},
Residing at Indiranagar, Bengaluru,
Pincode: {{PLACEHOLDER_PINCODE}}.

VERSUS

DEFENDANT:
MR. RAJESH REDDY,
Aged 60 years,
Occupation: {{PLACEHOLDER_OCCUPATION}},
Residing at HSR Layout, Bengaluru,
Pincode: {{PLACEHOLDER_PINCODE}}.

SUIT FOR SPECIFIC PERFORMANCE OF AGREEMENT TO SELL

FACTS

1. The Plaintiff is the absolute owner of the funds and is entitled to purchase the suit property. The Defendant is the absolute owner of the suit property described in the Schedule hereinbelow.

2. On 10.01.2024, the Plaintiff and the Defendant entered into an Agreement to Sell in respect of the suit property. The total consideration agreed upon was Rs. 85,00,000/-.

3. Pursuant to the Agreement to Sell dated 10.01.2024, the Plaintiff paid an advance amount of Rs. 25,00,000/- to the Defendant on the same date via RTGS. The UTR Number for the transaction is HDFC20240110987654.

4. As per the terms of the Agreement to Sell, the balance consideration of Rs. 60,00,000/- was to be paid by the Plaintiff on or before 10.07.2024 against the execution of the Sale Deed.

5. The Plaintiff has always been ready and willing to perform her part of the contract. The Plaintiff has arranged the funds for the balance consideration and is prepared to pay the same upon execution of the Sale Deed.

6. On 05.06.2024, the Defendant sent a letter to the Plaintiff refusing to execute the Sale Deed. The Defendant claimed in the letter that he had received a higher offer for the suit property.

7. On 20.06.2024, the Plaintiff issued a legal notice to the Defendant calling upon him to execute the Sale Deed as per the Agreement to Sell.

8. The Defendant refused to comply and reiterated his refusal to execute the Sale Deed.

9. The Plaintiff has performed her obligations under the Agreement to Sell by paying the advance consideration and remains ready and willing to pay the balance consideration.

10. The Defendant has breached the terms of the Agreement to Sell by refusing/repudiating performance and by failing to execute the Sale Deed despite the Plaintiff’s readiness and willingness.

LEGAL BASIS

11. The Plaintiff relies upon Section 10 of the Specific Relief Act, 1963, which states that specific performance shall be enforced subject to Sections 11(2), 14, and 16.

12. The Plaintiff relies upon Section 16 of the Specific Relief Act, 1963 regarding personal readiness and willingness to perform the contract.

13. The Plaintiff relies upon Section 21 and Section 22 of the Specific Relief Act, 1963 regarding compensation and other reliefs.

14. The Plaintiff relies upon Section 10 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 regarding valid contracts.

15. The Plaintiff relies upon Section 53A of the Transfer of Property Act, 1882 regarding part performance.

16. The Plaintiff relies upon Section 17(1A) of the Registration Act, 1908 regarding documents containing contracts to transfer immovable property.

CAUSE OF ACTION

17. The cause of action arose on 05.06.2024 when the Defendant, prior to the contractual date fixed for performance (10.07.2024), communicated refusal/repudiation by letter and declined to execute the Sale Deed.

18. The cause of action arose within the jurisdiction of this Hon'ble Court as the suit property is situated in Bengaluru and the parties are within jurisdiction.

19. The cause of action is continuing in view of the Defendant’s ongoing refusal to perform the Agreement to Sell, despite the Plaintiff’s readiness and willingness.

LIMITATION

20. The suit is governed by Article 54 of the Limitation Act, 1963.

21. The period of limitation is three years from the date fixed for performance or, if no such date is fixed, when the Plaintiff has notice that performance is refused. In the present case, though 10.07.2024 was the date fixed for performance, the Defendant’s refusal was communicated on 05.06.2024.

22. The present suit is filed within the prescribed period of limitation.

JURISDICTION

23. This Hon'ble Court has territorial jurisdiction as the suit property is situated within Bengaluru.

24. This Hon'ble Court has pecuniary jurisdiction to try the suit based on the valuation of the suit property.

25. The jurisdiction is invoked under Section 16 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.

VALUATION AND COURT FEE

26. The suit is valued for the purposes of jurisdiction at Rs. 85,00,000/- (Rupees Eighty-Five Lakhs only), being the total sale consideration agreed under the Agreement to Sell.

27. The suit is valued for the purposes of court fee at Rs. 85,00,000/-, and the court fee payable thereon is computed as per applicable court-fee law. Court fee of Rs. {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}}/- is paid herewith.

INTEREST

28. The Plaintiff is entitled to interest on the advance amount paid due to the Defendant's breach of contract.

29. The Plaintiff claims pre-suit interest if permitted by contract, usage, or substantive law.

30. The Plaintiff claims pendente lite and future interest under Section 34 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.

SCHEDULE OF PROPERTY

31. The suit property is described as follows:
RESIDENTIAL FLAT NO. 302, 3RD FLOOR, SUNSHINE APARTMENTS, KORAMANGALA, BENGALURU, KARNATAKA.

MEASURING 1200 SQ FT.

BOUNDARIES:
EAST: {{PLACEHOLDER_BOUNDARY_EAST}}
WEST: {{PLACEHOLDER_BOUNDARY_WEST}}
NORTH: {{PLACEHOLDER_BOUNDARY_NORTH}}
SOUTH: {{PLACEHOLDER_BOUNDARY_SOUTH}}

PRAYER

32. The Plaintiff prays for the following reliefs:
(a) Direct the Defendant to execute and register the sale deed in respect of the suit property in favour of the Plaintiff upon receipt of the balance consideration of Rs. 60,00,000/-;
(b) Direct the Defendant to deliver vacant and peaceful possession of the suit property to the Plaintiff (if not already in possession);
(c) In the alternative, award compensation under Section 21 of the Specific Relief Act, 1963 in lieu of or in addition to specific performance, at such amount as this Hon'ble Court deems fit;
(d) Award pre-suit interest if contract, usage, or substantive law permits, and pendente lite and future interest under Section 34 CPC as this Hon'ble Court deems fit;
(e) Award costs of the suit to the Plaintiff;
(f) Grant such other and further relief(s) as this Hon'ble Court deems fit and proper.

LIST OF DOCUMENTS (ANNEXURES)

33. The following documents are filed as annexures:

34. Agreement to Sell dated 10.01.2024 (Original) - Annexure P-1

35. RTGS Receipt for advance payment (UTR: HDFC20240110987654) - Annexure P-2

36. Legal Notice dated 20.06.2024 - Annexure P-3

37. Postal Receipt for delivery of legal notice - Annexure P-4

38. Defendant's refusal letter dated 05.06.2024 - Annexure P-5

VERIFICATION

39. I, SMT. ANITA SHARMA, the Plaintiff herein, do hereby verify that the contents of paragraphs 1 to 10 and 17 to 19 are true and correct to my personal knowledge.

40. I further verify that the contents of paragraphs 11 to 16 and 20 to 38 are based on legal advice/information received and believed by me to be true.

41. Verified at Bengaluru on this {{DATE}} day of {{MONTH}}, 2024.

PLACE: BENGALURU

DEPONENT
(SMT. ANITA SHARMA)

COUNSEL FOR PLAINTIFF
{{ADVOCATE_NAME}}
Advocate
Enrollment No. {{ADVOCATE_ENROLLMENT}}
{{ADVOCATE_ADDRESS}}

scenarios.name: Breach of Contract — Damages (Business)

checks.detail: Total claim Rs.32L or breakdown stated

scenarios.draft_text:
IN THE COMMERCIAL COURT AT BENGALURU, KARNATAKA STATE
Original Suit No. {{SUIT_NUMBER}} of 2024

BETWEEN

TechSoft Solutions Pvt Ltd, a company registered under the Companies Act, having its registered office at MG Road, Bengaluru, Karnataka, {{PIN_CODE}}, represented by its Director Priya Menon, aged 40 years, Occupation: Business.

... PLAINTIFF

AND

CloudBridge Systems LLP, a limited liability partnership having its office at Whitefield, Bengaluru, Karnataka, {{PIN_CODE}}, represented by its Designated Partner Vikram Singh, aged 48 years, Occupation: Business.

... DEFENDANT

SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF DAMAGES FOR BREACH OF CONTRACT

FACTS

1. The Plaintiff is a company registered under the Companies Act, engaged in the business of software solutions. The Plaintiff is represented by its Director, Priya Menon, who is authorized to file this suit.

2. The Defendant is a limited liability partnership engaged in the business of software development. The Defendant is represented by its Designated Partner, Vikram Singh.

3. On 01.02.2024, the Plaintiff entered into a written Software Development Agreement with the Defendant for the development of an ERP system. The total contract value was agreed at Rs. 45,00,000/-.

4. Pursuant to the agreement, the Plaintiff paid Rs. 20,00,000/- as the first milestone payment to the Defendant on 01.02.2024 via NEFT. The transaction bears UTR Number ICIC20240201456789.

5. Under the terms of the agreement, the Defendant was obligated to deliver the first module of the ERP system by 01.05.2024.

6. The Defendant failed to deliver any module by the agreed deadline of 01.05.2024.

7. On 15.06.2024, the Defendant abandoned the project entirely without completing the work and without returning the advance payment made by the Plaintiff.

8. Due to the non-delivery and abandonment of the project, the Plaintiff suffered additional loss of Rs. 12,00,000/- on account of delay in operations.

9. The Plaintiff issued a legal notice to the Defendant dated 01.07.2024 demanding refund and damages. The Defendant failed to comply with the notice.

10. The Plaintiff has quantified the total damages at Rs. 32,00,000/-, comprising Rs. 20,00,000/- as refund of advance and Rs. 12,00,000/- as consequential damages.

11. The Plaintiff has paid ad valorem court fee on the claim amount.

LEGAL_BASIS

12. The Plaintiff relies upon Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 for compensation for loss or damage caused by breach of contract.

13. The Plaintiff relies upon Section 37 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 as the Defendant failed to perform its reciprocal promise to deliver the module.

14. The Plaintiff relies upon Section 55 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 as time was fixed for the performance of the obligation.

15. The Plaintiff relies upon Section 20 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 for jurisdiction.

CAUSE_OF_ACTION

16. The cause of action arose on 15.06.2024 when the Defendant abandoned the project and breached the contract.

17. The cause of action arose within the territorial limits of Bengaluru since the Defendant carries on business within Bengaluru at its office at Whitefield, Bengaluru. Without prejudice, and to the extent relevant, the Plaintiff states that the Software Development Agreement was executed at {{PLACE_OF_EXECUTION}} and the agreed place of performance/delivery/acceptance/use of the ERP modules was {{PLACE_OF_PERFORMANCE}}.

18. The cause of action is continuing as the damages remain unpaid.

LIMITATION

19. The suit is governed by Article 55 of the Limitation Act, 1963.

20. The limitation period is three years from the date the contract is broken.

21. The suit is filed within the prescribed limitation period.

JURISDICTION

22. This Hon'ble Court has territorial jurisdiction under Section 20 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 as the Defendant carries on business within Bengaluru at Whitefield, Bengaluru. Without prejudice, and to the extent applicable, the agreement was executed at {{PLACE_OF_EXECUTION}} and performance/delivery/acceptance was at {{PLACE_OF_PERFORMANCE}}.

23. This Hon'ble Court has pecuniary jurisdiction as the value of the suit is Rs. 32,00,000/-.

24. This is a commercial dispute within the meaning of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015. The specified value for the purposes of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015 is Rs. 32,00,000/-, being the value of the relief claimed in the present suit.

PRE-INSTITUTION MEDIATION (SECTION 12A, COMMERCIAL COURTS ACT, 2015)

25. The Plaintiff states that it has complied with Section 12A of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015 prior to institution of the present suit and has obtained a non-starter report dated {{PIMS_REPORT_DATE}} bearing reference/number {{PIMS_REPORT_NO}}. In the alternative (if applicable), the Plaintiff seeks exemption from Section 12A on the ground that urgent interim relief is required for the following reasons: {{SECTION_12A_EXEMPTION_GROUNDS}}.

VALUATION_COURT_FEE

26. The suit is valued at Rs. 32,00,000/- for the purposes of jurisdiction and court fee.

27. Court fee of Rs. {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}} has been paid on the plaint.

INTEREST

28. The Plaintiff claims pre-suit interest at the rate of 18% per annum from 15.06.2024 till the date of filing of the suit.

29. The Plaintiff claims pendente lite and future interest under Section 34 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.

PRAYER

The Plaintiff respectfully prays for:

(a) decree for damages in the sum of Rs. 32,00,000/- on account of breach of contract (S.73 ICA)

(b) pre-suit interest at the rate of 18% per annum from 15.06.2024 till date of filing (Contract / usage / substantive law)

(c) pendente lite interest at such rate as this Hon'ble Court deems fit from filing till decree (S.34 CPC)

(d) future interest at such rate as this Hon'ble Court deems fit from decree till realisation (S.34 CPC)

(e) costs of the suit including advocate's fees (S.35 CPC)

(f) such other and further relief(s) as this Hon'ble Court may deem fit and proper

DOCUMENTS_LIST

30. Software Development Agreement dated 01.02.2024 (Annexure P-1)

31. NEFT receipt (UTR: ICIC20240201456789) dated 01.02.2024 (Annexure P-2)

32. Email correspondence showing abandonment (Annexure P-3)

33. Loss assessment report by auditor (Annexure P-4)

34. Legal notice dated 01.07.2024 (Annexure P-5)

35. Section 12A Commercial Courts Act, 2015 pre-institution mediation non-starter report dated {{PIMS_REPORT_DATE}} / reference {{PIMS_REPORT_NO}} (if applicable) (Annexure P-6)

VERIFICATION

I, Priya Menon, Director of TechSoft Solutions Pvt Ltd, the Plaintiff herein, do hereby verify and declare that:

36. Paragraphs 1 to 11 of the Facts section are true to my personal knowledge and belief.

37. Paragraphs 1 to 4 of the Legal Basis, Cause of Action, Limitation, Jurisdiction, Valuation Court Fee, and Interest sections are based on information received from my legal counsel and are believed to be true.

38. The Prayer and Documents List are true to my knowledge.

Verified at Bengaluru on this {{DATE}} day of {{MONTH}},

2024.

STATEMENT OF TRUTH (ORDER VI RULE 15A, CODE OF CIVIL PROCEDURE, 1908 — COMMERCIAL DISPUTE)

I, Priya Menon, daughter of {{FATHER_NAME}}, aged about 40 years, Director of TechSoft Solutions Pvt Ltd, having office at MG Road, Bengaluru, Karnataka, do hereby state that I have read and understood the contents of this plaint, and that the statements made therein are true and correct to my knowledge, and where based on information, I believe the same to be true. No part of it is false and nothing material has been concealed therefrom.

Signed at Bengaluru on {{DATE}}/{{MONTH}}/2024.

PLACEHOLDER_SIGNATURE
(Priya Menon)
Director for Plaintiff

COUNSEL FOR PLAINTIFF
{{ADVOCATE_NAME}}
Advocate
Enrollment No. {{ENROLLMENT_NO}}
Address: {{ADVOCATE_ADDRESS}}
Bengaluru, Karnataka
